coredata.dc:title: Amorphous fraction controlled mechanical and optical properties of polylactic acid below glass transition temperature
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Tsung-Han
Su, Yu-Hsun
Huang, Hsuan-Hao
Tsai, Hsin-Jung
Hsu, Wen-Kuang
coredata.dc:description: A negative relation between strength and crystallinity is observed in polylactic acid below glass transition temperature. Study indicates that entangled molecules in amorphous regions act as load bearing structures and are responsible for stress induced crazing. A three-phase model is proposed to explain how amorphous fraction changes with heat treatments and contributes to polymer modulus below glass transition temperature. Incorporation of carbon quantum dots into amorphous fraction dominated PLA creates a new type of luminescent composites that can be used for food labelling, tracking, packaging and production of origin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polylactic acid
Crystallinity
Strength
Amorphous fraction
Crazing
Chain mobility

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Optical images of PLA films with HT increasing from left to right. (b–c) Polarized light images obtained from samples 1 and 5 at magnification of × 40, × 200 and × 500 (from left to right).
(a) DSC profiles of sample 1–5 and (b) highlighted Tg, (c) Tc and (d) Tm.
(a–b) Stress-strain profiles of sample 1–5 obtained from bending (full and enlarged scale) and (c) tensile tests. Insert shows enlarged scale from square area.
DMA profiles of samples 1 and 5. (a) Storage modulus, (b) loss modulus and (c) Tanδ.

Three phase model (f
c -f
a -RAF) proposed to explain origin of strength enhancement below Tg for sample 1.

(a) FTIR spectra of samples 1 and 5, (b) highlighted 923 cm−1 profiles, (c) the C=O stretching modes of samples 1 (red, top) and 5 (dark, lower); the green and blue curves represent C=O mode splitting at 1771 and 1749 cm−1, (d) highlighted raman profiles at 409/394 cm−1 and (e) highlighted IR profiles at 1180/1082 cm−1.

(a) Optical images of straws 1 and 5. (b) Optical images obtained from creased region (circle) of bent straw 1; the lower inset shows unfolded straw 1. (c) Polarized light images obtained from straw 1 prior to bending. (d) Polarized light image obtained from creased regions of bent straw 1.
(a–d) SEM images obtained from crazed zone at × 50, × 100, × 130 and × 200 magnification. (e–f) SEM images obtained from heat treated straw at × 30 and × 100 magnification.
(a–b) HRTEM image of a CQD and corresponding electron diffraction. (c–d) Tissue paper supported CQDs without/with irradiation by 365 nm UV light.

(a–c) Optical pictures and 365 nm excitation spectra of CQDs-sample 1 and 5. (d–f) Optical pictures and 395 nm excitation spectra of CQDs-sample 1 and 5. (g–h) Screen printed trademark on PLA film without/with irradiation by 365 nm UV light.
(a) FTIR spectra of CQDs-sample 1 (blue), pure CDQs (red) and sample 1 (dark). (b) Comparison between sample 1 and CQDs-sample 1 where blue-shift takes place at C=O, CH3, C-O and OH groups.
Calculated particle sizes according to XRD.

head.ce:title: Amorphous fraction controlled mechanical and optical properties of polylactic acid below glass transition temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Tsing-Hua University, Hsinchu, 30013, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Tsing-Hua University, Hsinchu, 30013, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A negative relation between strength and crystallinity is observed in polylactic acid below glass transition temperature. Study indicates that entangled molecules in amorphous regions act as load bearing structures and are responsible for stress induced crazing. A three-phase model is proposed to explain how amorphous fraction changes with heat treatments and contributes to polymer modulus below glass transition temperature. Incorporation of carbon quantum dots into amorphous fraction dominated PLA creates a new type of luminescent composites that can be used for food labelling, tracking, packaging and production of origin.

A contrary relationship is realized between strength and crystallinity of PLA below glass transition temperature.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para:
Crystallization is a spontaneous process occurred to reduce free energy. Accordingly, metallurgy and metal processing often involve various types of heat treatments (HT) that facilitate atomic diffusion to the lowest energy positions in Bloch function. Polymers however are long-chain molecules oriented in all directions so crystallization is localized where moieties of adjacent molecules align. The remaining chains then entangle between crystalline regions (f
c ), forming so-called amorphous fractions (f
a ) [1]. Mechanical measurements indicate that most of semicrystalline polymers display Cn ∝ T
h (or Cn ∝ E) where Cn, T
h and E denote crystallinity, toughness and elastic modulus [2]. The f
a on the other hand behaves as damper against molecular mtovements according to two-phase (spring-dashpot) model Eηdε/dt = Eσ +ηdσ/dt where σ is stress, ε is strain and the η is viscosity coefficient arising from f
a [3]. Recent studies confirm existence of the 3rd phase (so-called rigid amorphous fraction, RAF) which is partially embedded in f
c thus acting as extension of rigidity [4,5]. RAF is immobile and contributes to E only as its dimension is comparable with lamellae [6].

Polylactic acid (PLA) is a biodegradable thermoplastic made from renewable feedstock. Because of its possible potential in replacing petro-based polymers the PLA industry has now focused on facile production, molding, surface modification and strength improvement [7]. Lactic acid is a chiral molecule with three stereoisomers (PLLA, PDLA and PDLLA) where L and D denote levorotatory and dexterotatory and, the PDLLA is a racemic mixture. Optical structures however make crystallization difficult thus a low T
h is expected according to Cn ∝ T
h [8]. On one occasion in examining PLA films the flexibility and rigidity are observed in optically transparent samples made by hot-press/air cool procedures. Films made by hot-press/HT on the other hand are fragile and show a milky appearance (video 1-2, supporting information). This outcome is fascinating because it contradicts Cn ∝ T
h and Cn ∝ E described above [9]. A series of experiments is therefore carried out to uncover (i) Cn at various lengths of HT and (ii) Cn-E relations below and above glass transition temperature (Tg). Results support video footages and confirm E ∝ Cn −1 below Tg. Study further reveal that entangled molecules in amorphous regions act as load bearing structures and are responsible for stress induced crazing. A three-phase model is proposed to explain how amorphous fraction varies with HT and contributes to polymer strength below Tg. Introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into f
a governed PLA creates a new type of luminescent composites that can be used for food labelling, tracking, packaging and production of origin.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.polymertesting.2020.106731

The following is/are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 2
Videos 1 and 2 demonstrate bending of PLA films with low and high Cn.
Multimedia component 2
Multimedia component 3 Multimedia component 3

Five different samples are made according to lengths of HT. Sample 1 is produced by melting PLA chips (d-lactide content = 99%, 6.5 g, Mw = 1.7–1.8 × 105, type Supla 018, YYC Material Technology CO, LTD) in a metal mold (100 × 20 × 3 mm) at 240 °C, followed by quenching in cold water (i.e. without HT). Sample 2–5 is made with HT where the temperature is fixed at 80 °C and length of HT is 1h for sample 2, 2h for sample 3, 3h for sample 4 and 24h for sample 5. For production of luminescent composites, CQDs are firstly prepared according to reported data [10–12]. CQDs are then mechanically blended with PLA chips in acetone (CQDs: PLA = 1 : 99 ratio, w.t%), followed by solvent evaporation at 40 °C and melting mixture at 240 °C. Two kinds of luminescent composites are produced and are hereafter defined as CQDs-sample 5 and CQDs-sample 1; the former is made with HT (80 °C, 24 h) and water cooling for the latter. It is worthy mentioing that samples 1 and 5 represent lower and upper bounds of Cn-HT relation in current study and are therefore loaded with CQDs for a comparison. Emission spectra of luminescent composites are recorded at 365 and 395 nm excitations.

The Cn, Cn ∝ HT, Tg and melting pointing (Tm) of PLA are characterized by polarized optical microscopy, powder x-ray diffraction (XRD, Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å, 45 kV, 40 mA Bruker, D2-PHASER,), differential scanning calorimetry (DSC, Netzsch, 200F3, 25–200 °C, heating/cooling rate = 5 °C/min), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Jasco, 4200) and confocal micro-raman spectroscopy (LABRAM HR 800 UV, Horiba Jobin Yvon, 632.8 nm excitation wavelength). Mechanical properties of PLA films are tested using dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA, frequency = 1 Hz, chuck distance = 8 mm, 25–120 °C, heating rate = 3 °C/min, TA instrument/Q800) and Instron-4468 (sample gauge = 30 mm, crosshead speed = 1 mm/min and pressing angle = 90°). Surface textures of sample 1–5 are inspected by polarized light microscopy and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, SU8010, 15 keV accelerating voltage).

Fig. 1 a shows optical images of samples made with and without HT, along with polarized light images obtained from samples 1 and 5 for a comparison (Fig. 1b and c). Clearly, HT reduces light transmittance and, the longer the HT the lower the transmittance (sample 2 → 5). Sample 1 on the other hand is transparent, indicative of low Cn. Cn ∝ HT is further evident by polarized optical images that exhibit a low and high number density of light-reflecting particles in samples 1 and 5 respectively (Fig. 1b and c). XRD verifies light-reflecting features to be α-crystallites with dimension around 15–30 nm according Scherrer's equation Ps = (0.9 × λ)/(dcosθ) where Ps is particle size, λ is wavelength of CuKα1 (= 1.5406 Å), θ is diffraction angle and d is full width at half maximum intensity extracted from [200] and [014] (2θ = 16.5° and 18.8° (Fig. 2 & Table 1 ) [13]. The milky appearance is a result of volume scattering rather than surface reflections from micro-pores and roughness; first, Ps ≪ visible wavelength indicates scattering to be Rayleigh type; second, film becomes transparent as XRD profiles vanish (Ps ~ 0, Fig. 2). Since samples are crystallized in quiescent condition the annealing kinetic that leads to increased multiplicity of Bragg planes and volume fraction of phase is mainly governed by enthalpy of fusion [14]. Accordingly, one can calculate Cn based on change of enthalpy ΔH
R = ΔH
heating + ΔH
cooling where ΔH
R is residual enthalpy of fusion at temperature (T1) below crystallization (Tc). ΔH
heating and ΔH
cooling are change of enthalpy upon heating and cooling; the latter corresponds to heat capacity of f
a between T1 and T2 where T2 > Tm [15]. Fig. 3 shows DSC of heating and cooling, along with estimated Cn. First, Cn ∝ HT is again verified (Fig. 3a). Second, intensity of Tg and Tc peaks gradually decreases with Cn increasing (i.e. sample 1 → 5), indicative of reduced f
a and chain mobility (Fig. 3b and c). Third, incorporation of chains into existing f
c is difficult so Tc(sample2-5) lies above Tc(sample-1) (Fig. 3c) [16]. Four, formation of α′- and α-crystallites results in asymmetric Tm profile; the former takes place at a lower T and is consistent with report (Fig. 3d) [17].

Fig. 4 a displays results of three-point bending measurements and highlighted profiles at low stress (Fig. 4b). All samples exhibit linear profiles at < 25 MPa, followed by yielding at 19 and 31 MPa for samples 3 and 4 and breaking at 36, 59 and 63 MPa for samples 5, 1 and 2. Table 2 lists mechanical parameters extracted from Fig. 4a where Y
s is yield strength and, the bending modulus (E) and flexural strength (f
s ) are calculated according to E = L3F/4wh3d and f
s = 3FL/2wh2 (L, w and h denote sample length, width and thickness). Since all samples are made in a similar size the parameters that actually determine magnitude of E and f
s appear to be load (F) and beam deflection (d). Clearly, samples 1 and 2 show superiority in all tests; the former is however 16% greater than the latter. Tensile tests give a similar result with ultimate strength and Y
s measured to be 33 and 21 MPa for sample 1 and, 20 and 15 MPa for sample 2 (Fig. 4c). At proportional limit, the E follows the sequence E
sample-1 > E
sample-4 > E
sample-2 > E
sample-3 where E
sample-1 lies on a range comparable with nylon (1–1.2 GPa) (insert, Fig. 4c). Sample 5 on the other hand fails at low stress (<5 MPa), again supporting E ∝ Cn −1 relation.

DMA is a technique widely used to characterize effects of temperature and frequency on viscoelastic body with stress and strain barely in phase. Tg hence phase lag can be determined according to dynamic moduli (E = E’ + iE’‘) and Tanδ where E′ and E″ represent storage and loss modulus and E’‘/E’ = Tanδ. Fig. 5 a and b shows E′ and E″ profiles of samples 1 and 5, along with Tanδ curves (Fig. 5c). Obviously, data complies with Fig. 4 and E' sample-1 > E' sample-5 prevails below T = 63 °C (Fig. 5a). E′ then drops at 63–72 °C, ascribed to glass transition and increased chain mobility [5]. The E″ complements E′ and is low for both E’‘ sample-1 and E’‘ sample-5 at T < Tg (25–30 MPa, Fig. 5b). E″ then increases rapidly at T ~ Tg, indicative of damping phase. At T ~ Tc, molecules become largely spaced so damping is significantly reduced (i.e. E″ drop). Tanδsample-1 > Tanδsample-5 again verifies Cn(sample-1) < Cn(sample-5) (or f
a(sample-1) > f
a(sample-5)) (Fig. 5c). It is noteworthy in spring-dashpot model that E is mainly contributed by f
c thus giving E ∝ f
c . Here E' sample-1 > E' sample-5 and Cn(sample-1) < Cn(sample-5) below Tg clearly indicate E ∝ f
a . Above 70 °C, E ∝ f
c dominates, accounting for E' sample-1 < E' sample-5 (Fig. 5a). Recrystallization of sample 1 is again supported by increased Tanδ at 100–110 °C (Fig. 5c). Since brittleness originates from lack of chain mobility [18] the E ∝ f
a below Tg may be explained according to RAF = 1 - f
c - f
a (%) or f
a = 1 – (f
c + RAF) (%) where f
c is considered as spherulites/framed springs (left & middle, Fig. 6 ) [4–6]. The f
a and RAF are on the other hand treated respectively as elastic springs and interlinks between f
a and f
c . The magnitude of f
a is then determined by two factors; the 1st is related to size of spherulites which dilate upon chain incorporation (f
a changes into f
c ). As a result, RAF prevails over inter-crystallite matrix and polymer becomes lack of elasticity (left, Fig. 6). The 2nd concerns the number density of spherulites (Sn) and, the greater the Sn the lower the f
a , consistent with optical images (Fig. 1 & right, Fig. 6).

FTIR and raman support reduced chain mobility in f
c based on peak intensity (i) and position (ii) (Fig. 7 a) [19]. The (i) includes IR peaks at 923 and 1771/1747 cm−1 and raman peaks at 394 and 409 cm−1. The 923 cm−1 comes from interaction between CH3 and C-C backbone and is strong as coupling takes place at f
c (Fig. 7b) [19]. Both 1771 and 1749 cm−1 are splitting mode of C=O; the former exists in f
c and therefore lies at higher frequency. The latter is C=O stretching in f
a where interaction is weak. Accordingly, f
a reduction leads to increase/decrease in 1771/1749 cm−1 peak intensity (Fig. 7c) [19]. Raman also verifies coupling induced deformation of C=O mode in f
c (394/409 cm−1, Fig. 7d) [20]. The (ii) is due to group coupling in f
c so peaks slightly blue-shift, including 1180 and 1082 cm−1 (Fig. 7e).

Polymer crazing is a specific kind of deformation induced by solvent effect or localized stresses. Craze precedes material failure and improves T
h through formation of bridging fibrils across micro-cracks [21]. Study indicates that bridging fibrils are few microns in length and contain a high density of chain entanglements (CEs) oriented in normal to stressing direction [22]. Since crazing and CEs only occur in glassy polymers samples here with low Cn should craze upon bending. Fig. 8 a shows optical images of drinking straws made from samples 1 and 5 (defined as straws 1 and 5 with thickness = 0.2 mm). A small bending causes an instant fracture to straw 5, consistent with video 2. Straw 1 however does not break but folded upon bending, attributable to crazing (insert, Fig. 8b). First, craze induces formation of micro-cracks that reduce refractive index of strained area, thus whitening creased regions (circles, Fig. 8b–d) [20]. Second, mechanical property is truly improved (Table 2) [21]. Third, SEM images verify formation of bridging fibrils and micro-cracks; the former lies in perpendicular to cracking direction (Fig. 9 a–d). Unlike cracking that leads to failure, polymer crazing is reversible through (i) HT above Tg and (ii) application of compressive stress [23]. Videos 3 and 4 demonstrate such a reversible process through hot water treatments (80–90 °C). First, whitened creases disappear and optical transparency recurs. Second, cracks disappear after HT (Fig. 9e and f).
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.polymertesting.2020.106731

The following are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 4
Videos 3 and 4 demonstrate crazing of sample 1 made drinking straw and recovery by heat treatment.
Multimedia component 4
Multimedia component 5 Multimedia component 5

Barcode and quick response code (QR code) are optical labels widely used in process management, admission control, labelling and packaging of merchandise. However, they can be copied and counterfeited without distortion through screenshot. Here we demonstrate for the first time the luminescent trademarks made of CQDs and f
a governed PLA; they are eco-friendly, recyclable, optically stealthy and are only excited by specific energy [10–12]. It is noteworthy that stilbene-, benzimidazole-, coumarin-, II-VI, III-V and IV-VI groups based luminescent systems lack thermal stability, particularly the issue concerning carcinogenicity remains to be addressed [24,25]. Fig. 10 a shows HRTEM image of a CQD (circle) and electron diffraction (Fig. 10b). CQD structure is verified by (i) layered structures along c-axis; (ii) 3.4 Å interlayer spacing; (iii) regularity arising from zigzag (1100) and armchair edges (1010) along basal planes [26]; (iv) comparable size with report (~10 nm) [11]; (v) UV luminescent (Fig. 10c and d). CQDs remain luminescent in PLA as evident by 365 nm excitation where emission wavelength peaks at 430 nm (Fig. 11 a–c). Emission intensity of CQDs-sample 5 however is low, ascribed to light scattering by larger f
c . The 395 nm excitation yields a similar result except emission maximum shifts to 510 nm for CQDs-sample 1 and 450 nm for CQDs-sample 5; the former is stronger and therefore governs coloration of composite films (Fig. 11 d-f). Screen printing of CQDs onto PLA film gives a similar effect where trademark is only visible under UV irradiation (Fig. 11g and h). Finally, it is important to understand how CDQs interact with PLA since both are highly oxygenated [11]. Fig. 12 a and b shows IR of CQDs-sample 1, along with spectra obtained from pure CQDs and sample 1 for a comparison. First, similar spectra between sample 1 and CQDs-sample 1 indicate that physical blending does not create chemical bonds. Second, profiles of C=O, C-O, OH and CH3 blue shift by 5–8 cm−1, indicative of enhanced coupling upon dipole-dipole interactions [19,20].

Mechanical properties of PLA films are realized inversely proportional to Cn below Tg, including E, Y
s , f
s and T
h . CEs in f
a act as load bearing structures and are responsible for crazing. A three-phase model is proposed to explain Cn ∝ E
−1 and Cn ∝ T
h
−1 below Tg. Combination of low Cn PLA and CQDs creates a new type of luminescent composites that can be used for food labelling, tracking and packaging and production of origin.
Tsung-Han Yu: Investigation, Data curation. Yu-Hsun Su: Resources. Hsuan-Hao Huang: Validation. Hsin-Jung Tsai: Project administration. Wen-Kuang Hsu: Writing - original draft, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors thank the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan for the financial support 108-2112-M-007 -008. This work is also supported by “High Entropy Materials Center” from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan (108QR001J4).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new method to quantify crazing in various environments
